title: Broccoli and Stilton soup
subtitle: Rich, savoury, and deeply British
excerpt: Blended broccoli and leek form the mild, bright base; a generous crumbling of Stilton stirred in at the end gives the soup its personality. Classic British partnership, ready in 35 minutes.
sourceNotes: Broccoli and Stilton as a pairing is well established in British cookery writing from the 1980s and 1990s. No single canonical public-domain source; method synthesised from the standard British puréed soup approach.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the base up to two days ahead and refrigerate without the Stilton. Add Stilton when reheating.

glossaryTerms.definition: A protected-designation British blue cheese with a crumbly, creamy texture and strong, salty, slightly tangy flavour, made in Derbyshire, Leicestershire, and Nottinghamshire.

content.text: Broccoli and Stilton is one of the best pairings in British cooking. The mild, grassy broccoli sets off the sharp, salty cheese, and neither drowns the other. The soup takes 35 minutes and needs very little skill: good cheese and a stick blender.

items.prepNote: white and pale green parts, sliced
peeled and cut into chunks (for body)
about 600 g, roughly chopped, stem and florets

content.text: Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{leek}} leek with a pinch of salt. Cook for 8 minutes until soft.
Add the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the {{potato}} of potato chunks and {{broccoli}} broccoli. Pour over {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 15 minutes until everything is tender.
Remove from the heat. Blend with a stick blender until smooth.
Return the pan to a low heat. Add {{stilton}} of crumbled
and stir until melted in. Add {{double-cream}} of cream if using. Taste: you may not need additional salt as the cheese is already salty.
Ladle into bowls. Crumble a little extra Stilton over each portion and add a twist of black pepper.

items.symptom: Soup turns grey-green rather than bright green
items.fix: Keep the cooking time to 15 minutes maximum. The broccoli should be tender, not mushy. Longer cooking destroys the chlorophyll and turns the colour dull.

items.cause: The quantity was too high for the mild broccoli base, or the Stilton was very aged and sharp.
items.fix: Start with 80 g, blend, taste, and add more to your preference. The amount in this recipe suits a standard young Stilton; reduce if using a particularly strong piece.

items.cause: Not enough potato to give body, or the broccoli stems were too coarse.
items.fix: Use the broccoli stem as well as the florets. The stem blends more smoothly than the florets alone. Make sure the potato is completely soft before blending.

items.symptom: Soup splits or looks curdled after the Stilton is added
items.cause: The soup was too hot when the cheese was added, causing the fat to separate.
items.fix: Take the pan off the heat before stirring in the Stilton. Stir steadily rather than whisking, and keep the heat very low when you return it to warm through.

content.text: Broccoli and Stilton soup belongs to the tradition of using strong British cheese to carry a mild vegetable base, in the same way that cauliflower cheese uses a béchamel. Stilton is one of England's great protected cheeses, made in three Midlands counties, and this soup is one of the most efficient ways to use it. It appears on pub lunch menus across the country, usually in a brown bread roll that costs half as much again.
